pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Broadening automated eval coverage without tighter review structure can reduce reviewer efficiency, because larger runs made it harder to distinguish repeated failure patterns from one-offs; smaller reviewed batches produced more actionable grouped issues (e.g., repeated citation problems).
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly says the broad run produced more scores but reviewers had to rediscover whether failures were new, while the smaller batch made repeated citation issues easier to group and produced more actionable prompt changes.

claimJudgments.claimText: Coverage gains can coincide with weaker grounding, so citation support should be an explicit shipping gate rather than inferred from aggregate coverage/score improvements.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 directly states coverage can improve while grounding regresses, that citation support needs an explicit shipping gate, and that coverage and grounding must be tracked separately.

claimJudgments.claimText: Long traces are useful for diagnosing failures, but uncontrolled retries increased cost in the last dry run (1.34x); trace retention is acceptable if retries are capped and retry/cost context is summarized in the run manifest.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 explicitly supports all parts: long traces helped debug/diagnose failures, unchecked retries caused a 1.34x cost increase, and finance accepted trace retention if retries are capped and retry count/cost ratio are summarized in the manifest.

claimJudgments.claimText: Holdout cases should be visible only as summaries/aggregate scores in dashboards, while keeping labels/expected answers out of prompt-iteration workflows to reduce memorization risk.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 states holdout cases should appear in dashboards as summaries and aggregate scores, with tuning labels and expected answers excluded from prompt-iteration workflows to avoid memorizing visible examples/overfitting.

claimJudgments.claimText: A prudent near-term approach is one more visible batch before live orchestration, expanding only if grounding improves without exceeding cost guardrails, while maintaining citation gating, manifest retry reporting, and aggregate-only holdout reporting.
claimJudgments.explanation: S6 directly recommends one more visible batch before live orchestration, citation support as a shipping gate, retry counts in the manifest, aggregate-only holdout performance, and expansion only if grounding improves without exceeding the cost guardrail.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation directly addresses whether to expand automated evals and balances the requested dimensions: coverage should not broadly expand yet; citation support should be a release gate; traces should be retained for debugging with retry/cost controls; and holdouts should remain aggregate-only. The constraints are well supported by S1-S4 and S6. S5 would additionally support the dashboard clustering emphasis, but its omission does not materially weaken the recommendation because similar clustering/actionability points are supported by S1.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
